title: Making pre-felt sheets
subtitle: How to wet-felt roving into thin, stable pre-felt sheets for cutting, appliqué, and picture work
excerpt: Pre-felt is wool that has begun to bond but is not yet fully dense. Sheets can be cut cleanly with scissors, overlaid for colour blending, and applied to a host felt without distorting. This guide covers making consistent pre-felt sheets from merino roving.
sourceNotes: Pre-felt sheet making is standard practice in flat-felt work and felt appliqué. Technique synthesised from general wet-felting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool that has begun to bond and holds together as a sheet but is not yet fully felted. It can be cut cleanly, applied to other felt surfaces, and further felted in place.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, the starting material for wet felting.
Alternating horizontal and vertical layers of roving placed to ensure the finished felt has equal strength in all directions.
Vigorous agitation that compresses and shrinks wool felt to its final density. Pre-felt is deliberately stopped before full fulling to keep the sheet workable.

content.text: sheet is wool that has been wet-felted far enough to hold together without support but not so far that it has become a dense, finished felt. The fibres are bonded enough that the sheet cuts cleanly with scissors, but the surface is still slightly fuzzy and open, which means it can be applied to a background felt and felted further in place. Pre-felt is the raw material for cut-out appliqué motifs, layered colour blending, and picture-making.
Lay the felting mat on a flat surface. Pull thin, even wisps of

to cover an area approximately 30 × 30 cm (for a pre-felt sheet used in appliqué, two layers are usually enough; for a sturdier sheet that will be cut into pieces and stretched, three or four layers are better). Each layer should be thin enough to see through slightly. Keep the layers even throughout: thick patches create hard spots in the finished sheet.

Mix a few drops of olive-oil soap into warm water (approximately 40°C). Pour the soapy water slowly and evenly over the roving until every fibre is saturated. Place the felting net over the surface and press down gently with flat hands to push out air bubbles. The roving should flatten slightly and the layers should compact together.

Through the felting net, rub the surface with flat hands using small circular movements. Begin with very light pressure for 2 minutes. Increase to medium pressure for a further 3 minutes. The surface will begin to form a skin. Lift the net and test the sheet by pinching a small area: if the fibres pull away as loose strands, continue rubbing. When pinched fibres hold together and the sheet lifts as a single piece from the mat, the sheet has reached pre-felt stage.

A correctly made pre-felt sheet can be cut with scissors without the edges fraying. It is pliable and slightly stretchy. It should not be dense or stiff. If the sheet has been rubbed too long and has begun to

(it becomes noticeably thicker and less stretchy), it is still usable as a finished felt panel but will not bond as well to a background felt during appliqué. Stop rubbing as soon as the sheet lifts from the mat in one piece.

Rinse the pre-felt sheet in cool water. Do not wring. Lay it flat on a dry towel and blot the surface. Leave flat to dry at room temperature. Do not tumble dry or hang: both will distort the sheet. Store flat between sheets of parchment paper or in a folder. Pre-felt sheets can be stored dry for months without change.

Cut shapes from the dry pre-felt sheet using scissors or craft punches. Apply the cut shapes to a damp background felt and rub them gently into the surface. The fuzzy cut edges will bond to the background during further felting and the join will become invisible. For picture work, build up layers of different-coloured pre-felt shapes from the background colour outward, felting each layer before adding the next.

items.problem: The sheet tears when lifted from the mat
items.cause: The roving was not rubbed for long enough before removing the net
items.fix: Return the sheet to the mat, re-wet, replace the net, and rub for a further 3-4 minutes. Check the bond test again before lifting.

items.problem: The sheet has thick and thin patches
items.cause: The roving layers were uneven during laying
items.fix: Pull the roving into thinner wisps before laying and build up layers gradually. Holding the wisp up to a light source and pulling it thinner until it is almost transparent produces even layers.

items.cause: The sheet was under-processed and the fibres on the cut edge have not bonded
items.fix: Re-wet the sheet and rub for a further 2-3 minutes. A correctly made pre-felt should cut cleanly with fabric scissors without any fraying.
